title: Oliver Twist
author: Charles Dickens
prompt:
Question: Which of the following passages is verbatim from the "Oliver Twist" book by Charles Dickens?
Options:
A. If I had that thought, I'd never be happy again. I wouldn't prevent a boy, not even Brittles, for all the silver in the county. The doctor mysteriously said that wasn't the issue. He asked Mr. Giles if he was Protestant. Giles nervously said yes, he hoped so. The doctor sharply turned to Brittles and demanded what he was. Brittles was startled and said he was the same as Giles. The doctor told them to swear whether the upstairs boy was put through the window last night. He angrily demanded they confess as they were ready for them! Giles and Brittles were confused by ale and excitement, staring blankly at each other.
B. I would be miserable forever if I had that thought. I wouldn't stop a boy, not even Brittles, for all the plate in the county, sir. The doctor said mysteriously that wasn't the point. He asked Mr. Giles if he was Protestant. Giles faltered that yes, he hoped so. The doctor turned sharply on Brittles and demanded what he was. Brittles started violently and said he was the same as Mr. Giles. The doctor told them both to swear whether the boy upstairs was the one put through the little window last night. He angrily said to confess as they were prepared for them! Giles and Brittles were muddled by ale and excitement, and stared stunned at each other.  
C. If I thought it, I should never be happy again. I wouldn't cut a boy off: no, not even Brittles here; not for all the plate in the county, sir.' 'That's not the point,' said the doctor, mysteriously. 'Mr. Giles, are you a Protestant?' 'Yes, sir, I hope so,' faltered Mr. Giles, who had turned very pale. 'And what are YOU, boy?' said the doctor, turning sharply upon Brittles. 'Lord bless me, sir!' replied Brittles, starting violently; 'I'm the same as Mr. Giles, sir.' 'Then tell me this,' said the doctor, 'both of you, both of you! Are you going to take upon yourselves to swear, that that boy upstairs is the boy that was put through the little window last night? Out with it! Come! We are prepared for you!' The doctor, who was universally considered one of the best-tempered creatures on earth, made this demand in such a dreadful tone of anger, that Giles and Brittles, who were considerably muddled by ale and excitement, stared at each other in a state of stupefaction.
D. If I had that thought, I would never find happiness again. I wouldn't prevent a boy, not even Brittles, from something; not for all the silverware in the county. The doctor mysteriously said that wasn't the issue. He asked Mr. Giles if he was Protestant. Giles nervously confirmed he hoped so. The doctor sharply questioned what Brittles was. Brittles was startled and said he was the same as Giles. The doctor demanded they both swear whether the boy upstairs was the one put through the small window last night. He angrily told them to confess as he was ready for their response. Giles and Brittles were very confused from the ale and excitement, and stared blankly at each other.
Answer:

He asked Mr. Giles if he was Protestant. Giles nervously said yes, he hoped so. The doctor sharply turned to Brittles and demanded what he was. Brittles was startled and said he was the same as Giles. The doctor told them to swear whether the upstairs boy was put through the window last night.
